1: diōkete tēn agapēn (pursue love): diōkō = to pursue, chase after — the same verb used for persecution, here applied to the active pursuit of love. The imperative is urgent and directional: love is not passively received but actively sought. Zēloute de ta pneumatika (eagerly desire spiritual gifts): zēloō = to be zealous, to burn with desire — the echo of 12:31 now contextualized within love as the operative mode. Mallon de hina prophēteuēte (especially that you may prophesy) — the preference for prophecy over tongues is the chapter's central argument.
2: ho gar lalōn glōssē ouk anthrōpois lalei alla Theō (the one speaking in a tongue speaks not to people but to God): the directional problem with tongues-without-interpretation — the communication is vertical (God-ward) but the assembly context requires horizontal (community-ward) intelligibility. Pneumati de lalei mystēria (but in the spirit he speaks mysteries): mystēria = hidden things, not yet intelligible; the tongue-speaker accesses divine reality but cannot convey it to others without interpretation.
3: ho de prophēteuōn anthrōpois lalei oikodoomen kai paraklēsin kai paramythian (the one who prophesies speaks upbuilding, encouragement, and consolation to people): three terms that together describe prophecy's communal function. Oikodomē = construction, building up — Paul's key criterion for all communal practice. Paraklēsis = encouragement, exhortation (the same root as Paraclete — the Spirit's own name). Paramythia = consolation, comfort — used here only in Paul; a distinctly pastoral function.
4: ho lalōn glōssē heauton oikodomeī (the one speaking in a tongue builds himself up): heauton = himself — the reflexive pronoun marks the gift's self-directed benefit. Ho de prophēteuōn ekklēsian oikodomeī (the one who prophesies builds up the church): the community-directed benefit. Paul is not denigrating self-edification but arguing from the assembly context: gathered worship is primarily for communal building, not individual spiritual enrichment. Both are real goods; in the communal setting, communal benefit takes priority.
5: thelō de pantas hymas lalein glōssais (I want all of you to speak in tongues): Paul's surprising affirmation — he is not opposed to tongues, he desires it universally. Mallon de hina prophēteuēte (but more that you prophesy) — the comparative mallon (more, rather) sets up the preference without absolute prohibition. Ektos ei mē diermēneuē (unless he interprets): the crucial condition that makes tongues communally equivalent to prophecy — interpretation renders them equivalent in function.
6: ti hymas ōphelēsō ean mē hymin lalēsō ē en apokalypsei ē en gnōsei ē en prophēteia ē en didachē (how will I benefit you unless I speak to you in revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching?): ōpheleō = to benefit, profit — the utilitarian criterion is communal benefit. The four modes of intelligible speech (revelation, knowledge, prophecy, teaching) represent the range of Spirit-given communication that edifies; uninterpreted tongues falls outside all four categories for the assembly's purpose.
7: ta apycha phōnēn didonta (lifeless things giving forth sound): apychos = without soul/life (the compound of a-privative + psychē). Musical instruments — flute (aulos) and harp (kithara) — make sounds that require musical structure (intervals, melody) to be music rather than noise. Pōs gnōsthēsetai to auloumenon ē to kitharizomenon (how will what is played on the flute or harp be known?) — the musical analogy: undifferentiated sound is unintelligible regardless of the instrument's inherent quality.
8: ean gar adēlon salpinx phōnēn dō (for if the trumpet gives an indistinct sound): salpinx = battle trumpet; adēlon = unclear, uncertain — the negation of dēlos (evident/clear). In ancient warfare, trumpet calls had specific meanings (advance, retreat, rally) — an unclear call would produce confusion rather than coordinated action. Tis paraskeuasetai eis polemon (who will prepare for battle?) — the military analogy captures the life-or-death stakes of intelligible communication in the assembly context.
9: houtōs kai hymeis dia tēs glōssēs ean mē eusoēmon logon dōte (likewise with you, if through the tongue you do not give intelligible speech): eusoēmos = well-signaling, distinct, clear — eu (well) + sēma (sign/signal). Eis aera laleite (you are speaking into the air): the devastating summary — uninterpreted tongue-speech passes through the assembly space without landing anywhere. The air receives it; no human mind is addressed or built up. The image reinforces the directional critique of v. 2.
10: tosauta ei tychoi genē phōnōn estin en kosmō (there are perhaps so many kinds of sounds in the world): ei tychoi = as it happens, whatever the number may be — a casual epistemic hedge. Genē = kinds, varieties. Kai ouden aphōnon (and none of them is without meaning): aphōnos = voiceless, without meaningful sound — Paul's point is that all languages in the world, however foreign-sounding, have communicative structure for their speakers. The tongue-speaker's sounds are similarly structured for God but unintelligible to the assembly.
11: ean oun mē eidō tēn dynamin tēs phōnēs (if I do not know the meaning/force of the sound): dynamis tēs phōnēs = the power/force of the voice — the communicative capacity of a given speech-form. Barbaros tō lalounti (a foreigner to the speaker) — barbaros = one whose speech sounds like 'bar-bar' to Greek ears — the onomatopoeic term for unintelligible foreigner-speech. The mutual barbarian-status: both parties are unintelligible to each other when there is no shared linguistic code. The assembly context requires a shared code.
12: zēlōtai este pneumatōn (you are zealots for spiritual manifestations): zēlōtēs = one who burns with zeal (the political Zealots took this name for themselves). Paul acknowledges the Corinthian enthusiasm as real but misdirected. Pros tēn oikodomēn tēs ekklēsias zēteite hina perisseūēte (seek to abound for the upbuilding of the church) — the redirect: the same zeal should be directed toward gifts that build the community. Perisseúō = to abound, overflow — Paul wants the Corinthians' enthusiasm channeled toward communal benefit.
13: ho lalōn glōssē proseuchesthō hina diermēneuē (let the one speaking in a tongue pray that he may interpret): the gift-pairing requirement — the tongue-speaker should seek the complementary gift of interpretation. Diermēneuō = to interpret, translate (the dia-compound intensifies: to translate thoroughly). The prayer for the interpretive gift is itself an act of communal orientation: the tongue-speaker is learning to value the community's understanding as much as his own spiritual experience.
14: ean gar proseuchomai glōssē to pneuma mou proseuchetai ho de nous mou akarpos estin (for if I pray in a tongue my spirit prays but my mind is unfruitful): to pneuma mou = my spirit — the human spirit engaged in prayer. Ho de nous mou = but my mind (the rational faculty). Akarpos = unfruitful, without fruit — the mind produces no communicable understanding during tongue-prayer. The cognitive dimension of prayer (which shapes, forms, and communicates) is bypassed. Paul does not dismiss spirit-prayer but insists the mind must also be engaged.
15: proseuxomai tō pneumati proseuxomai de kai tō noi (I will pray with the spirit and I will pray with the mind also): the both-and resolution — neither spirit-prayer alone nor mind-prayer alone, but both together. Psallō tō pneumati psallō de kai tō noi (I will sing with the spirit and sing with the mind also) — the singing dimension: psallō = to sing psalms/hymns (originally to pluck a stringed instrument). The principle applies to all devotional acts: full engagement of spirit and mind together is the Pauline standard.
16: epi tō sō eucharistia (at your giving of thanks): eucharistia = thanksgiving — the term used for the eucharistic prayer and for table-blessings. Idiōtēs (the ungifted person/layperson): idiōtēs = a private person, one who does not hold a public role — in this context, one who does not have the gift of tongues or interpretation. Pōs erei to amēn (how will he say Amen?) — the congregational Amēn was the community's ratification of a prayer; inability to understand the prayer-content makes ratification impossible.
17: sy men gar kalōs eucharisteis all' ho heteros ouk oikodomeītai (for you give thanks well enough, but the other person is not being built up): the men/de (you... but the other) contrast is precise: Paul concedes the spiritual quality of the tongue-speaker's thanksgiving while insisting the communal building-up criterion is not met. Kalōs (well) — the tongue-prayer is genuinely good in itself; the problem is not its quality but its location and context.
18: eucharistō tō Theō pantōn hymōn mallon glōssais lalō (I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you): Paul's astonishing personal disclosure — his tongue-speaking exceeds even the Corinthians' practice. This is not modesty but strategic rhetoric: his argument against tongues in the assembly cannot be dismissed as the complaint of someone who lacks or devalues the gift. He has it; he surpasses them in it; and he still insists on intelligible speech in the gathering.
19: en ekklēsia thelō pente logous dia tou noos mou lalēsai (in the assembly I would rather speak five words with my mind): the dramatic ratio — five intelligible words vs. ten thousand in a tongue. Dia tou noos mou (through my mind) — mind-engaged speech, not bypassing rational faculties. Hina kai allous katēchēsō (so that I might instruct others also) — katēcheō = to instruct orally (origin of English 'catechism'); the oral-instruction model for assembly speech. Myrious logous en glōssē = ten thousand words in a tongue — the hyperbole underlines the inversion of Corinthian values.
20: mē paidia ginesthe tais phresin (do not be children in your thinking): phrenes = mind, practical reason — the cognitive faculties as a whole. Alla tē kakia nēpiazete (but in evil be infants): a paradox — Paul wants Corinthian immaturity reversed in cognition while recommending a childlike inexperience in evil. Tais phresin teleioi ginesthe (but in your thinking be mature): teleios = complete, mature — the same term as 13:10 and 2:6. Gift-competition and tongues-preference are symptoms of cognitive immaturity; the corrective is the mature mind that evaluates gifts by communal benefit.
21: en tō nomō gegraptai (it is written in the Law): the citation is from Isa 28:11-12, referred to as 'the Law' — Paul uses nomos broadly for all Scripture. The Isaiah text describes YHWH speaking to the northern kingdom en heteroglōssois (in foreign tongues/languages) — a sign of judgment addressed to those who refused to hear plain speech. Paul's typological argument: tongues function as a sign for unbelievers (in the Isaianic sense — a sign of judgment on the unbelieving), while prophecy functions for believers.
22: hōste hai glōssai eis sēmeion eisin ou tois pisteuousin alla tois apistois (so then tongues are a sign, not for believers but for unbelievers): sēmeion = sign — a significant action with referential meaning beyond itself. The Isaiah use of foreign-tongue speech was a judgment-sign to the unbelieving nation; tongues function analogously as a sign-act. Hē de prophēteia ou tois apistois alla tois pisteuousin (but prophecy is not for unbelievers but for believers) — the complementary assignment: each gift has a different primary audience and function.
23: ean oun synelthē hē ekklēsia holē epi to auto (if then the whole church comes together in one place): epi to auto = in one place, together — the full-assembly scenario. Pantes glōssais lalousin (all speak in tongues): the maximalist tongues-scenario produces the perception of madness. Mainesthe = you are out of your mind — the visitor's verdict uses the same term used for Paul's supposed madness before Festus (Acts 26:24). Unintelligible speech is indistinguishable from madness to the outsider.
24: ean de pantes prophēteuōsin (but if all prophesy): the contrasting scenario — universal prophecy in the assembly produces a different dynamic. Elenchetai hypo pantōn (he is convicted by all) — elenchō = to expose, convict, reprove (the Spirit's own work in John 16:8). Anakrinetai hypo pantōn (he is examined/discerned by all) — anakrinō = to examine carefully. The unbeliever who enters a prophesying assembly undergoes a Spirit-driven examination of his inner life.
25: ta krupta tēs kardias autou phanera ginetai (the secrets of his heart are made manifest): krupta = hidden, secret things (the inner life); phanera ginetai = become visible/evident — the prophetic word exposes what was concealed. The eschatological imagery: the Day of the Lord will manifest all hidden things (1 Cor 4:5); prophecy anticipates that disclosure in the present assembly. Pesōn epi prosōpon proskunēsei tō Theō (falling on his face he will worship God): the conversion described through the posture of submission — the involuntary prostration before divine reality.
26: hekastos psalmon echei, didachēn echei, apokalypsin echei, glōssan echei, hermēneian echei (each has a psalm, a teaching, a revelation, a tongue, an interpretation): the charismatic plurality of the Corinthian assembly — Paul describes it without dismissal. The five-item list represents the range of contributions members bring. Panta pros oikodomēn ginesthō (let all be done for building up) — the single criterion repeated: the communal building function is the test for any contribution.
27: eite glōssē tis lalei, kata duo ē to pleiston treis (if anyone speaks in a tongue, let it be two or at most three): the quantitative limit — not prohibition but regulation. Ana meros (each in turn) — sequential, not simultaneous; the orderly queue of speakers. Kai heis diermēneuetō (and let one person interpret): a single interpreter for all tongue-speakers in the session, ensuring coherence rather than competing interpretations.
28: ean de mē ē diermēneutēs, sigatō en ekklēsia (but if there is no interpreter, let him be silent in the assembly): sigatō = let him be silent — the same verb as in v. 34 (women's silence). The silence is conditional on the absence of interpretation, not absolute. Heautō de lale tō theō (let him speak to himself and to God) — the private exercise of the gift remains valid; the assembly context requires the interpretive pair.
29: prophētai de duo ē treis lalesatōsan kai hoi alloi diakrinetōsan (let two or three prophets speak and let the others weigh/discern): diakrinetōsan = let them discern/distinguish (aorist imperative of diakrino) — the community's testing of prophetic utterances. This is the diakrisis pneumatōn (discernment of spirits) of 12:10 exercised in the assembly setting. Prophecy is not self-validating; it requires communal evaluation.
30: ean de allō apokalythptē kathēmenō, ho prōtos sigatō (but if something is revealed to another sitting there, let the first be silent): the mechanics of ordered prophetic exchange. Apokalyptō = to reveal — the prophetic insight arrives to a seated listener during another's speech. The first speaker must yield: the gift does not override the will, and the prophet can control his speech. This directly counters any claim of overwhelming spiritual compulsion that cannot be regulated.
31: pantes gar dynasthe kath' hena prophēteuein, hina pantes manthanōsin kai pantes parakalōntai (for you can all prophesy one by one, so that all may learn and all may be encouraged): kath' hena = one by one, sequentially — the possibility of universal participation regulated by order. Manthanōsin (learn) — prophecy has a didactic function alongside its experiential one. Parakalōntai (be encouraged/exhorted) — the same root as paraklēsis in v. 3: prophecy's pastoral function recapitulated in the purpose clause.
32: kai pneumata prophētōn prophētais hypotassetai (and the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets): pneumata prophētōn = the spirits of prophets — either the human spirit of the prophets or the prophetic impulse. Hypotassō = to arrange under, subject to — the claim that the prophetic endowment does not override the prophet's volitional control. This refutes any 'I couldn't help myself' defense for disorderly behavior. The prophet remains the agent; the Spirit equips without coercing.
33: ou gar estin akatastasias ho Theos alla eirēnēs (for God is not a God of disorder but of peace): akatastasia = instability, disorder, tumult (used in Jas 3:16 alongside jealousy and selfish ambition). The theological argument from God's character: ordered assembly worship reflects the orderliness of God himself. Hōs en pasais tais ekklēsiais tōn hagiōn (as in all the churches of the saints) — the universality claim: Corinth's practice should conform to the pattern of all Paul's churches.
34: hai gynaikes en tais ekklēsiais sigatōsan (women should be silent in the assemblies): one of the most debated commands in the NT. The connection to the orderly-assembly context and the parallel with tongues-silence (v. 28) and prophets yielding (v. 30) suggests the silence is contextual (evaluating prophecy? disruptive questioning?) rather than absolute. Textual issues: some manuscripts place vv. 34-35 after v. 40, suggesting possible interpolation. The command may be directed at a specific Corinthian disruption pattern: wives questioning their husbands' prophesying in the assembly.
35: ei de ti mathein thelousin, en oikō tous idious andras eperotatōsan (if they want to learn something, let them ask their own husbands at home): eperotatōsan = let them ask/question — the verb is for interrogation, inquiry. En oikō = at home — the private domestic sphere as the appropriate venue for women's theological questions. Aischron gar estin gynaiki lalein en ekklēsia (for it is shameful for a woman to speak in the assembly) — aischron = shameful, disgraceful; the cultural shame-honor dynamic invoked for the assembly context.
36: ē aph' hymōn ho logos tou Theou exēlthen (or did the word of God originate from you?): the rhetorical rebuke addressed to a self-important community that thinks it can set its own norms. Ē eis hymas monous katēntēsen (or has it come to you alone?) — the double rhetorical question attacks Corinthian exceptionalism: they did not generate the gospel and they are not its sole possessors. Both questions are answered with an implied 'no', deflating any claim to authority over the practice of all the churches.
37: ei tis dokei prophētēs einai ē pneumatikos, epiginsketō ha graphō hymin hoti kyriou estin entolē (if anyone thinks he is a prophet or spiritual, let him acknowledge that what I write to you is a command of the Lord): the test of genuine spiritual maturity is recognition of apostolic authority. Dokei = considers himself, seems to himself — the self-evaluation. Entolē kyriou (a command of the Lord) — Paul's instructions carry dominical authority. The irony: the truly Spirit-filled prophet recognizes the Spirit speaking through the apostle.
38: ei de tis agnoei, agnoeitai (but if anyone ignores this, he is ignored): agnoeō = to be ignorant of, to ignore — the double use creates a lex talionis structure: the one who ignores (the apostolic command) is himself ignored (by God). The passive agnoeitai may be a divine passive: God does not recognize the one who refuses to recognize the Spirit's ordering of the community. The eschatological stakes of the communal-order discussion are thereby revealed.
39: hōste adelphoi, zēloute to prophēteuein kai to lalein glōssais mē kōlyete (so then, brothers, eagerly desire to prophesy and do not forbid speaking in tongues): the balanced conclusion. Zēloute = eagerly desire (the imperative of the verb used positively throughout the chapter for the right kind of gift-seeking). Mē kōlyete = do not forbid — kōlyō = to prevent, hinder. The two prohibitions/permissions are carefully balanced: prioritize prophecy, but do not suppress tongues. Paul's pastoral wisdom avoids both extremes: tongues-mania and tongues-prohibition.
40: panta de euschēmonōs kai kata taxin ginesthō (but let all things be done properly and in order): euschēmonōs = properly, becomingly, fittingly — from euschēmōn (well-formed, honorable, cf. 12:24). Kata taxin = according to order — taxis is the military/civic ordering term. The summary principle governs all that has come before in chs. 12-14: gift diversity, love's primacy, and orderly assembly practice all derive from God's own character (v. 33: 'God is not a God of disorder'). The ordered assembly is itself a theological statement about the God who is worshipped.
